messages.content:
Hi everyone,

The leaderboard for Mathematics Distillation Challenge - Equational Theories: Stage 1 has been released. You can now view your results here:

https://competition.sair.foundation/leaderboard/mathematics-distillation-challenge-equational-theories-stage1

There are two leaderboards. The main scored leaderboard is based on 600 problems: 200 easy, 200 hard, and 200 extra-hard problems. The order-5 leaderboard is provided for research purposes.

The evaluation problem sets are available on Hugging Face:

https://huggingface.co/datasets/SAIRfoundation/equational-theories-selected-problems

The corresponding subsets are evaluation_normal, evaluation_hard, evaluation_extra_hard, and evaluation_order5.

You can use the filters on the leaderboard to inspect how your cheatsheet performed on each model and each problem set. Different models and problem sets have different winners, so we hope these results will help participants learn from one another and prepare for Stage 2.

Please note that only cheatsheets submitted through the official submission portal were evaluated and counted for the final leaderboard. Cheatsheets tested only in the Playground are not included.

Because the evaluation and checking process had limited time, and because LLM outputs can vary slightly across repeated runs, some small score differences may occur. We hope you understand, and we have tried to make the process as consistent and fair as possible.

For discussion about insights from the results, please use this channel. If you have questions about your team's score, feel free to DM me.
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content: Go team! Some great results on there. Congratulations to Dufius, vt and Reza - top effort!
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content:
Thanks to the organizers for running this, I really enjoyed digging into the problem. One pattern from the leaderboard that caught my eye: many of the top extra-hard performers scored lower on normal and hard. I ran some analysis with my programatic checker on top cheatsheets (and my own) and the mechanism is interesting.

  1. The evaluation extra hard cheat sheet seems to fit into a very narrow set of problems. Across all rules on the cheat sheets I tested, the majority of them never fired on any problems. However, there were some that fired a lot:
  • vt and pi both had a rule called ‘H3’ which captured all 100 of the FALSE problems in the extra hard set. When tested on the full ETP, however, this rule only had 17.3% precision!
  • AJ had a witness magma called M7 which is sound (100% precision everywhere), but the eval set selected for it heavily: 18.5% of evaluation hard problems and 34.5% of extra-hard problems vs 0.17% of the natural 22M distribution, a ~200x concentration. On Hard2 and Hard3 this only covered 3% of problems.
  • On my own cheatsheet, I had a heuristic called ‘F1’. This achieved > 70% accuracy on all other problem sets but falls to 0% on 65 problems in extra hard.
  1. It seems that different problem sets had different rules that worked. All cheat sheets seem to perform better or worse on a different set of them. Evaluation is a genuinely hard problem. Perhaps for the next stage there should be more (but smaller) problem sets constructed with different methods:
    evaluation hard.png

Note:

  • Since I only tested the top cheat sheets, it could be the case that these just so happened to have rare rules that fit the evaluation set. If we look further down the leaderboard, we might see a greater diversity of rule fires. Perhaps all problems sets will have certain rules that spike disproportionately. In a competitive format this seems like an issue, but it is unclear to me how or if this can be resolved. I’d be curious if people extend this analysis to more cheat sheets and see what they find.
  • I would also be really interested to know what the problem selection methodology for the eval and practice sets were. It was mentioned that Hard3 and the evaluation extra hard had different ones.

Implementation caveats:

  • eucalyptus's "Classification" step is intuition-only; it was collapsed into CLASSIFY_DEFAULT_FALSE per the cheatsheet's own "If uncertain, FALSE" hint. I am curious as to how well the models were able to intuit at this step. 
  • aj's Step 3 (bilinear matrix magmas), Step 4 (partial subterm algebra), and Step 5 (perturbation method) are not finite-Cayley-table primitives and were not implemented.
  • yan-biao's B_SUB (substitution unification) runs scalar on the small splits but is skipped (zero-mask) for the 22M ETP run — per-pair tree unification is intractable in numpy. B3_LOOSE's "exactly one dirty slot" is approximated as ≤1.

Github with more details: https://github.com/arjun2garg/SAIR-Math-Distillation/

messages.content:
My team's cheatsheet had about 9% lower average accuracy on the official SAIR evaluation than on our test set of problems. It underperformed on the evaluation for all 3 models and all 3 difficulty levels. Parse rate didn't change, though.

How does this compare to your team's results?

messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content:
Congrats to all participants, certainly a new challenge for everyone involved, including the organizers. Happy to participate in collaborative mathematics, truly a 21st century event!

  • Team Costa Rica (#22 leaderboard, #10 GPT only)
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content:
Thanks Gan! Happy to share the methodology — implementation details matter less than the approach.
Three principles drove the cheatsheet:

  1. Profile before prompting. I cross-referenced the public sets against the full ETP ground truth to see which features actually discriminate TRUE/FALSE on the curated tiers. The classical filters (balance, leftmost-leaf, support) fire on ~0% of hard problems — those tiers are explicitly designed to defeat them. Building rules without checking what fires on real data leads to elegant but useless filters.
  2. Deterministic classifier, not free-form reasoning. Small models hallucinate proofs. So I wrote a rule engine: a mandatory parse step that extracts structural features first, then layered rules — identity/collapse → forced behavior → empirical contradiction motifs → separators → affine probes → heuristic rejects. The model never gets to "think" about a problem; it gets pushed through a decision tree. That's where most of the lift came from.
  3. Targeted fallback instead of default-FALSE. Instead of guessing FALSE when no rule fires, I added a small fallback layer that uses Eq1's variable-occurrence pattern (the minimum total count M, the LHS structure) to make a directed guess. On the tail of problems that escape all the strict rules, this seems to do better than a blind 50/50.
    Two affine probes (A1 and A7) got fast-path shortcuts because rightmost-leaf + right-depth arithmetic is something the model can execute reliably; the general coefficient-recursion is where it tends to slip.
    Curious to see Pi's and Paws' approaches — looks like they hit the same problem from very different angles. Looking forward to Stage 2.
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content:
Yes, this confirms the main Stage 1 lesson: the finding is not "LLMs have deep universal algebra intuition." The wins came from giving the LLM the missing metacognition it needed for the task.

A lot of us saw this early on and most of the top cheatsheets did this too - instead of asking the model to freely reason, make it behave like a disciplined symbolic router. Only possible with ground truth as reference point.

That is exactly why profiling the full ETP implication graph mattered. The useful rules were not elegant hand-written slogans or concepts, they were survivor patterns found across the law space. Strong FALSE cues gave the model something procedural and reliable to execute.

Stage 2 will be different, and the natural next level. Seems much closer to the deeper SAIR goal. A verdict is no longer enough, the system now has to produce a checkable mathematical object.

So I think the upgrade now is: router - constructor - verifier.

The router still matters, but its job changes. It should not just classify, its more important job is to select the right certificate-producing route.

That also feels aligned with Tao's bigger goal for this challenge: not just getting models to guess mathematical truth, but building systems that can distill mathematical structure into verifiable artefacts. Stage 1 tested whether language models could be disciplined into reliable mathematical judgment (they can with the right governance). Stage 2 tests whether that judgment can be turned into actual evidence.

In other words, Stage 1 was about routing truth. Stage 2 is about certifying it. Congrats again to all the winners. Looking forward to the next challenge, all the best everyone!
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content:
Congratulations to all teams with strong Stage 1 results. Thank you all for your participation and insightful discussions!

Stage 2 has officially launched, and we hope participants can build on what they learned from Stage 1 to develop strong solvers. Looking forward to seeing everyone’s work in Stage 2! Cheers! :tada: :tada:
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content: Thought I might share this, I'm sure some people noticed it: If full Jinja was allowed, symbolic reductions are very easy, wiping out most of the normal difficulty and even some hard ones. This is clearly cheating for the first round, but could save some tokens before actually feeding it to the model.
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content: congrats everyone! my super fitted prompts for a given dataset definitely didn't generalize ;p
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content: @Adam Betka Your work was one of the main sources for my sheets. I (Claude on my behalf) detailed how it was used in the message above. Thank you.
messages.subject: Stage 1 Leaderboard

messages.content: Oh this is interesting! I built my prompt just looking at ~all the equations~ of order 4 without selecting for easy/hard, just trying to get whatever got the best overall accuracy. But that put me at position 154 for "Normal", position 32 for "Hard", and position 6 for "Extra hard". Which is exactly the opposite of what I expected! I thought that, since I was looking for what /typically works/, that would it naturally end up aligning very much with normal. Huh!
